stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])x + ([[d]])y \). What is the rate of steepest ascent of \( f \) at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), i.e. the maximum rate of increase (the magnitude of the gradient there)?
solution: The gradient is \( \nabla f = \langle 2([[a]])x + ([[c]]),\ 2([[b]])y + ([[d]]) \rangle \), which at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) is \( \langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \). The rate of steepest ascent is \( \lVert \nabla f \rVert = \sqrt{[[p]]^{2} + [[q]]^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the gradient components instead of taking the magnitude.
This is the squared magnitude; the rate is its square root.
This adds absolute values of the components (and is off by one) rather than using the Pythagorean magnitude.
This doubles the correct magnitude.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])x + ([[d]])y \). The direction of steepest ascent of \( f \) at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) is the unit vector \( \hat{\mathbf{u}} = \langle u_{1}, u_{2} \rangle \) parallel to \( \nabla f([[x0]], [[y0]]) \). What is \( u_{1} \), the \( x \)-component of \( \hat{\mathbf{u}} \)?
solution: The gradient is \( \nabla f([[x0]], [[y0]]) = \langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \), which has magnitude \( \sqrt{[[p]]^{2}+[[q]]^{2}} = [[mag]] \). The direction of steepest ascent is \( \hat{\mathbf{u}} = \dfrac{1}{[[mag]]}\langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \), so \( u_{1} = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[mag]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the raw gradient component \( f_{x} \), not normalized to a unit vector.
This uses the \( y \)-component of the gradient instead of the \( x \)-component.
This has the wrong sign, giving the \( x \)-component of the steepest DESCENT direction instead.
This divides by the squared magnitude instead of the magnitude itself.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])x + ([[d]])y \). The rate of steepest descent of \( f \) at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) is defined as \( -\lVert \nabla f \rVert \) evaluated there (the most negative directional derivative). What is this rate?
solution: The gradient at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) is \( \langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \), with magnitude \( \sqrt{[[p]]^{2}+[[q]]^{2}} = [[m]] \). The rate of steepest descent is the negative of this magnitude, \( -[[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign, giving the rate of steepest ASCENT instead of descent.
This adds the gradient components instead of taking the negative magnitude.
This negates the sum of the components rather than the Pythagorean magnitude.
This doubles the correct magnitude before negating.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y, z) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + [[c]]z^{2} \). What is the rate of steepest ascent of \( f \) at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \), i.e. the magnitude of the gradient there?
solution: The gradient is \( \nabla f = \langle 2([[a]])x,\ 2([[b]])y,\ 2([[c]])z \rangle \), which at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \) is \( \langle 2([[a]])([[x0]]),\ 2([[b]])([[y0]]),\ 2([[c]])([[z0]]) \rangle \). The rate of steepest ascent is \( \lVert \nabla f \rVert = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the gradient components instead of taking their Pythagorean magnitude.
This is the squared magnitude; the rate is its square root.
This drops the \( z \)-component of the gradient entirely.
This adds absolute values of the components instead of using the Pythagorean magnitude.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + by^{2} \) for an unknown positive coefficient \( b \). At the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), the direction of steepest ascent of \( f \) is parallel to the vector \( \langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \). What is the value of \( b \)?
solution: The gradient is \( \langle 2([[a]])x,\ 2by \rangle \), which at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) must be a scalar multiple of \( \langle [[p]],[[q]]\rangle \). Matching the \( x \)-component fixes the scalar (since \( 2([[a]])([[x0]]) = [[p]] \) means the scalar is 1), so \( 2b([[y0]]) = [[q]] \), giving \( b = \dfrac{[[a]]([[x0]])([[q]])}{([[y0]])([[p]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the correct ratio, solving as if \( a \) were the unknown instead of \( b \).
This computes \( q/(2y_0) \) directly, forgetting that this equals \( b \) only after also accounting for the coefficient \( a \) via \( p \).
This computes \( p/(2x_0) \), which just recovers the KNOWN coefficient \( a \), not the unknown \( b \).
This forgets to divide by \( y_0 \), only scaling by \( p \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The elevation of a hillside at position \( (x, y) \) (in hundreds of meters east and north of a base camp) is \( h(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \). A hiker standing at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) wants to climb as steeply as possible. What is the rate of increase in elevation per unit distance (the magnitude of the gradient) in that best direction?
solution: The gradient is \( \nabla h = \langle 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y,\ 2([[b]])y + ([[c]])x \rangle \), which at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) is \( \langle 2([[a]])([[x0]]) + ([[c]])([[y0]]),\ 2([[b]])([[y0]]) + ([[c]])([[x0]]) \rangle \). The steepest rate of increase is \( \lVert \nabla h \rVert = [[answer]] \) (in hundreds of meters of elevation per unit of horizontal distance).
distractor_rationales: This adds the gradient components instead of taking their Pythagorean magnitude.
This is the squared magnitude; the rate is its square root.
This drops the mixed term \( [[c]]xy \)'s contribution to each gradient component.
This is the elevation \( h([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) itself, not the rate of steepest ascent.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])x + ([[d]])y \). The direction of steepest DESCENT of \( f \) at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) is the unit vector \( \hat{\mathbf{v}} = \langle v_{1}, v_{2} \rangle \) anti-parallel to \( \nabla f([[x0]], [[y0]]) \). What is \( v_{1} \), the \( x \)-component of \( \hat{\mathbf{v}} \)?
solution: The gradient is \( \nabla f([[x0]], [[y0]]) = \langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \), with magnitude \( [[mag]] \). The direction of steepest descent is the OPPOSITE unit vector, \( \hat{\mathbf{v}} = -\dfrac{1}{[[mag]]}\langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \), so \( v_{1} = -\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[mag]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the \( x \)-component of the steepest ASCENT direction instead, forgetting the sign flip for descent.
This uses the \( y \)-component of the gradient (negated) instead of the \( x \)-component.
This is the raw (negated) gradient component, not normalized to a unit vector.
This divides by the squared magnitude instead of the magnitude itself.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
